frontMatter.title: MA 104: Ordinary Differential Equations - Lecture 1

frontMatter.summary: This lecture introduces the fundamental concepts of Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs), including their definition, order, and various applications in modeling physical systems. It covers the general forms of first-order ODEs, discusses the existence and uniqueness of solutions, and explores specific types such as homogeneous and exact equations. Key methods for solving certain first-order ODEs are also introduced.

key_formulas.name: Definition of an Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE)
key_formulas.formula: An equation that contains one or more derivatives of an unknown function, usually denoted by $y(x)$ (or $y(t)$).
key_formulas.note: The order of an ODE is the highest derivative present in the equation.

key_formulas.name: Implicit Form of a First Order ODE
key_formulas.note: Where $y'$ is the first derivative of the unknown function $y$.

key_formulas.name: Explicit Form of a First Order ODE
key_formulas.note: This form is often easier to work with directly.

key_formulas.name: General Solution for $y' = f(x)$
key_formulas.note: When the derivative only depends on the independent variable.

key_formulas.name: Transformation for Homogeneous ODEs ($y' = g(y/x)$)
key_formulas.formula: Let $u = y/x$, then $y = ux$ and $y' = u'x + u$.
key_formulas.note: This substitution transforms the ODE into a separable one in terms of $u$ and $x$.

key_formulas.name: Exact Differential Equation Condition
key_formulas.formula: An equation $M(x, y) dx + N(x, y) dy = 0$ is exact if and only if $\frac{\partial M}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial N}{\partial x}$.
key_formulas.note: If exact, there exists a potential function $u(x,y)$ such that $du = M dx + N dy = 0$, leading to the general solution $u(x,y) = C$.
